signals.description: User is evaluating whether to enter or expand into a market and wants a read on structural attractiveness.

paradigms.rationale: Attractiveness is a structural-profitability question, which is the core of competitive analysis.
Entry also requires a credible read on opportunity size.

structures.rationale: Canonical structure for scoring industry forces and rivalry dynamics.
Frames internal fit against external opportunity and threat.

strategies.rationale: Attractiveness is meaningful relative to analogous markets and peer industries.
Use multiple lenses — forces, peers, customer — before committing.

signals.description: User wants to compare the company against specific named rivals on features, pricing, or positioning.

paradigms.rationale: Direct rival comparison is the narrow sub-question of competitive analysis.
Rivals often serve different segments; segmentation sharpens the comparison.

structures.rationale: Weighted scorecard across criteria is the natural teardown structure.
Side-by-side metric and feature comparison.

strategies.rationale: The whole point of a teardown is relative, not absolute, evaluation.
Comparison depends on which criteria the target customer actually weights.

signals.description: User is asking how large a market, category, or segment is — TAM/SAM/SOM style question.

paradigms.rationale: Core paradigm for quantifying opportunity.
Long-horizon sizing depends on reading structural trends in demand.

structures.rationale: Decomposes the size into population × penetration × price or analogous drivers.
Base/bull/bear ranges are often more useful than a point estimate.

strategies.rationale: Classic reconciliation strategy for any sizing exercise.
Multiple methodologies raise confidence in the estimate.

signals.description: User is deciding which sub-segment of a market to prioritize for product or go-to-market.

paradigms.rationale: Segment selection is the defining question of this paradigm.
Each candidate segment needs its own sizing.

structures.rationale: Rank segments by criteria such as size, fit, margin, and winnability.
Compare segment behavior on retention and monetization.

strategies.rationale: Prioritization requires explicit weights across criteria.
Segment quality is best read relative to the current base.

signals.description: User wants to know whether a marketing campaign actually drove incremental results.

paradigms.rationale: Incrementality is fundamentally a causal question.
Attribution feeds reallocation across channels.

structures.rationale: Cohorts by acquisition source reveal channel quality over time.
Captures spend against incremental revenue.

strategies.rationale: Lift only exists relative to a credible counterfactual.
Holdouts, MMM, and geo-lift should agree before acting.

signals.description: User is evaluating unit economics — customer acquisition cost, lifetime value, and payback period.

paradigms.rationale: CAC depends on funnel efficiency from impression to customer.
LTV is a forward projection of cohort cash flows.

structures.rationale: Retention and revenue cohorts are the canonical view for LTV.
Decomposes LTV into ARPU × gross margin × lifetime.

strategies.rationale: CAC and LTV only have meaning against category peers.
Activation and early retention lead eventual LTV.

signals.description: User is diagnosing where in an acquisition or sales journey volume is being lost.

paradigms.rationale: Direct fit for stage-by-stage drop-off diagnosis.
Fixing a stage requires experimental validation that changes helped.

structures.rationale: Conversion = product of stage conversion rates, naturally a tree.
Cohort-by-stage shows whether drop-off is worsening over time.

strategies.rationale: Upstream stages lead downstream revenue.
Isolates whether a fix caused the improvement.

signals.description: User is building a fair-value or target-price view on a public or private company.

paradigms.rationale: The defining paradigm for fair value.
Valuation rests on a forward free-cash-flow projection.

structures.rationale: Canonical intrinsic-valuation structure.
Relative-valuation anchor alongside the DCF.

strategies.rationale: DCF, trading comps, and precedents together bound the answer.
Identifies which assumptions dominate the target price.

signals.description: User is updating an equity view heading into or out of earnings, revisiting drivers and guidance.

paradigms.rationale: Estimate changes feed directly into fair-value revision.
Earnings update is primarily a revenue and margin re-forecast.

structures.rationale: Revise the revenue and margin tree branch by branch.
Tests which revised assumptions move the target price.

strategies.rationale: Ground the update in the filing and transcript, not just sell-side notes.
Compare results and guidance against peers and prior period.

signals.description: User is allocating capital across investments, projects, or marketing channels under a fixed budget.

paradigms.rationale: The paradigm is defined by constrained allocation across bets.
Each candidate needs a risk-adjusted return estimate.

structures.rationale: Weighted comparison across candidates under constraints.
Flags inputs whose uncertainty dominates the recommended mix.

strategies.rationale: Allocation must trade expected return against distribution of outcomes.
Explicit weights on return, risk, and strategic fit.

signals.description: User is stress-testing a plan, valuation, or portfolio against macro or exogenous uncertainty.

paradigms.rationale: Direct fit: the paradigm is defined by disciplined scenario construction.
Each scenario is a forecast under coordinated driver moves.

structures.rationale: Canonical structure for laying out the scenarios.
Identifies which drivers most warrant scenario-level variation.

strategies.rationale: Scenarios are the main vehicle for surfacing downside risk.
Complements scenarios by isolating the biggest levers.

signals.description: User is assessing a long-horizon structural trend in technology, demographics, regulation, or consumer behavior.

paradigms.rationale: The paradigm is defined by reading durable exogenous shifts.
Trends are usually assessed across multiple consistent futures.

structures.rationale: 2x2 of structural uncertainties is the standard framing.
Decomposes the trend into measurable sub-drivers.

strategies.rationale: Hiring, patents, filings, and search interest lead realized spend.
Trend narratives must be grounded in measurable primary data, not press.

signals.description: User is evaluating a proposed price change and its likely impact on volume, revenue, and churn.

paradigms.rationale: Price sensitivity is best measured via experiments or matched-market lift.
Revenue impact requires forward projection under new prices.

structures.rationale: Nets incremental margin against churn and volume loss.
Shows which elasticity assumptions drive the recommendation.

strategies.rationale: Measuring the price effect requires a credible no-change counterfactual.
Pricing balances customer, shareholder, and channel-partner interests.

signals.description: User is updating a revenue, demand, or cost forecast in light of new data or changed conditions.

paradigms.rationale: Direct fit — this is the core paradigm.
Segment-level forecasts are more robust than a pooled projection.

structures.rationale: Decomposes the forecast into drivers that can be updated independently.
Base/bull/bear communicates uncertainty around the point estimate.

strategies.rationale: Revised model should be validated on a holdout before use.
Top-down, bottom-up, and analog forecasts should be reconciled.

signals.description: User is positioning a portfolio, business, or decision against geopolitical shocks — war, sanctions, trade tensions, or supply-route disruption.

paradigms.rationale: Geopolitical shocks are best analyzed through disciplined scenario construction with coordinated driver moves.
Shocks sit atop longer-horizon structural shifts in alignment, trade, and energy flows.

structures.rationale: Canonical structure for laying out escalation/stalemate/de-escalation or similar branch points.
Decomposes shock pathways into sector-specific drivers.

strategies.rationale: Shock positioning must weigh expected outcomes against distribution of tail risk.
News, market prices, and official data should corroborate any scenario probability estimate.
Ground analysis in filings, regulator statements, and official data rather than narrative aggregators.

signals.description: User is assessing exposure to a commodity price spike or supply disruption — oil, gas, agricultural inputs — and how it flows through portfolios or margins.

paradigms.rationale: Commodity shocks are inherently scenario-driven: supply, demand, and policy branches.
Pass-through modeling requires explicit forward projection of price and pass-through coefficients.

structures.rationale: Base/bull/bear on commodity price with paired demand outcomes.
Decomposes impact into input cost, volume, and substitution channels.

strategies.rationale: Margin and revenue sensitivity to input cost is the load-bearing calculation.
Asymmetric payoff shapes call for risk-aware framing over point estimates.

signals.description: User is deciding which sectors to favor or avoid based on a macro or cycle thesis — risk-on vs risk-off, cyclicals vs defensives, or event-driven rotation.

paradigms.rationale: Rotation is a reallocation problem under a macro prior.
Sector theses rest on reading cycle, regime, or policy shifts.
Sector dispersion is anchored by relative valuation at entry.

structures.rationale: Ranks sectors by criteria such as earnings sensitivity, valuation, and positioning.
Side-by-side sector metrics — multiples, growth, beta — support ranking.

strategies.rationale: Rotation is meaningful relative to index weights or prior cycles, not in isolation.
Each sector tilt has an asymmetric payoff depending on which regime prevails.

signals.description: User wants a near-term trade idea or tactical tilt over the next quarter — buy/short/hedge calls with explicit entry and exit logic.

paradigms.rationale: Short-horizon calls live or die by correctly mapping the probable path and its catalysts.
Tactical positions must be sized against the rest of the book.

structures.rationale: Compact branch map of catalyst outcomes anchors entry and exit triggers.
Shows which catalyst or price level most moves the trade's expected value.

strategies.rationale: Tactical trades must have explicit asymmetry: bounded loss, quantified upside.
Historical analog fit should be validated out of sample before sizing.

signals.description: User is asking for tools, data sources, or screeners that can inform a decision — not an analysis itself, but the plumbing for one.

paradigms.rationale: Tool discovery is often in service of reading a macro signal from primary data.
Tools often exist to segment a universe (tickers, sectors, geographies) before analysis.

structures.rationale: Tool catalogs are naturally rendered as capability-vs-limit comparison tables.
When selecting among tools, a weighted matrix over coverage, cost, and access is the default structure.

strategies.rationale: Tool discovery is primarily about reaching primary data — regulators, exchanges, official statistics — over aggregators.
Useful tool stacks let the user cross-check data across independent providers.

antiOverlapRules.description: No two selected paths should share both paradigm and structure — diversity across paradigm or structure is required.
If two paths share the same paradigm, they must differ in at least two of {structure, primary strategy, secondary strategy}.
antiOverlapRules.check: For any pair sharing paradigmId, |symmetric_difference(components(path1), components(path2))| >= 2

antiOverlapRules.description: At least one selected path must use a strategy that forces external comparison (benchmark-comparison, triangulation, or primary-vs-secondary-sources) when the signal concerns valuation, market sizing, or competitive analysis.
antiOverlapRules.check: If signal ∈ {market-entry-attractiveness, market-size-estimate, equity-fair-value, earnings-thesis-update}, at least one chosen strategyId ∈ {benchmark-comparison, triangulation, primary-vs-secondary-sources}

antiOverlapRules.description: Scenario/shock-family signals must carry a risk-aware lens. When the matched signal is in {geopolitical-risk-positioning, commodity-shock-exposure, tactical-positioning-short-horizon, scenario-stress-test}, at least one chosen strategyId MUST be ev-vs-risk-framing OR sensitivity-analysis.
antiOverlapRules.check: If signal ∈ {geopolitical-risk-positioning, commodity-shock-exposure, tactical-positioning-short-horizon, scenario-stress-test}, at least one chosen strategyId ∈ {ev-vs-risk-framing, sensitivity-analysis}
